findings.message: Top-level JSON shape is not validated before calling `.get("$schema_version")`, so a syntactically valid but non-object document (for example `[]` or `"foo"`) is reported as `MissingKey { key: "$schema_version" }` instead of the documented parse/structure failure. That misclassifies a malformed config and makes the error contract inconsistent.

Gate-cell type errors fall through to `SignalGatesConfigError::ParseFailed { path: PathBuf::new(), ... }` because `find_invalid_strictness_cell` only checks string values and ignores numbers/nulls/arrays/objects. A config like `"adr_user": 1` therefore loses the real file path and offending key/value, violating the loader's error-reporting contract.

`read_adr_verify_report` now scans `repo_root/knowledge/adr` directly, but it no longer applies the trusted-root / symlink rejection that `execute_verify_adr_signals` uses. A branch can make `knowledge/adr` a symlink and redirect the merge-gate scan outside the repository, which is a path-traversal/security regression.

The new qualified-path branch accepts any `qself` path but only reads the single segment at `qself.position`. If the path has additional trailing segments after the associated item, they are silently dropped instead of remaining unresolved, so valid inputs can be misrepresented as a different type.

The new QualifiedPath tests only assert a few fields (`name`, presence of `trait_`, `args`) and never compare the full `Type::QualifiedPath` literal. That leaves the main D1 regression risk untested: the builder could still emit the wrong `self_type` or other nested shape and these tests would pass. The briefing explicitly called for full `assert_eq!` coverage here.

`read_adr_verify_report` is a new public merge-gate adapter entry point, but the added coverage only checks the symlink rejection path. There is no test for the expected success path or for a non-symlink I/O/parse failure, so a break in the new ADR scan wiring or error mapping would ship without regression coverage.

`Ok(false)` from `reject_symlinks_below` is not equivalent to a cleanly missing `knowledge/adr` directory. If `knowledge/` is replaced with a regular file or another non-directory ancestor, this branch returns `NotFound` and the merge gate will treat ADR scanning as absent, bypassing chain 0 instead of failing closed.

`read_adr_verify_report` delegates to `FsAdrFileAdapter`, which skips non-regular entries instead of rejecting them. That means symlinked `knowledge/adr/*.md` files are silently ignored, so a branch can hide all ADR decisions behind symlinks and get an empty clean report instead of a fail-closed error.

The new `TraitImplDeclV2.for_type` generic-param path is only covered by parser unit tests. There is no end-to-end codec regression test that exercises `encode(doc)` with `impl_generics = ["T"]` and `for_type = "T"`, so regressions in the new callsite plumbing or `normalize_impl_for_type_path` would not be caught.

`AssocTypeDecl.default` and `AssocConstDecl.ty` are only checked with `TypeRef::new`, which accepts any non-empty string. Unlike the other `TypeRef` fields in this codec, these values are never run through `validate_type_ref_str`, so malformed Rust types (for example `Vec<` or `Foo<`) now survive decode and fail later in the encode path instead of being rejected at the DTO boundary.

`AssocTypeDecl` defaults are parsed with `parse_type_ref_str` but never rewritten against `trait_generic_names`. That means defaults such as `type Item = Vec<T>` are still rendered with a raw `T` path instead of `Type::Generic("T")`, so traits that only differ by generic parameter renames will compare unequal even though the bounds path already canonicalizes those names.

The new assoc-item decoders only reject empty names. `assoc_types[*].name` and `assoc_consts[*].name` are never validated as Rust identifiers, and there is no duplicate-name check before the items are fed into `Trait.items` / `build_trait_method_map`. A malformed or colliding assoc item can therefore be accepted and silently overwrite a different item during trait fingerprinting, producing a false match.

`assoc_const_decl_from_dto` accepts `default_value: Some("")` with no validation. That collapses to the same fingerprint as `None` later (`unwrap_or("")` in trait comparison), so malformed wire input can silently masquerade as a missing associated-const value and change trait equality results.

`encode_assoc_type_item()` and `encode_assoc_const_item()` still call `parse_type_ref_str()` without any trait-generic context, then rely on `rewrite_generic_types()`. That rewrite does not descend into `Type::QualifiedPath`, so valid defaults/types like `T::Item`, `<T as Trait>::Assoc`, or `Vec<T::Assoc>` will keep a non-generic self-type and can be emitted as the wrong rustdoc shape. The new assoc-item codec will therefore mis-encode valid trait-generic inputs instead of matching the C-side representation.

This appends `AssocType`/`AssocConst` IDs into `Trait.items`, but the contract-map renderer still walks `Trait.items` and emits only `ItemEnum::Function` children. As a result, traits with associated items now influence equality/signals but those items remain invisible in rendered output, so the D4 change is internally inconsistent.

`validate_trait_item_names` now enforces a single flat name set across methods, associated types, and associated consts. That rejects valid traits that reuse an identifier across namespaces (for example, an associated type and a method with the same name), so the codec will fail on some legal catalogues instead of round-tripping them.

`validate_trait_item_names` now accepts legal cross-namespace name reuse (`type Item; fn Item();` and `type Item; const Item: ...;`), but the trait comparer still flattens every trait item into a single `BTreeMap<String, String>` keyed only by bare `name`. Once assoc types/consts are emitted into `Trait.items`, those legal traits become order-dependent or collide during comparison, so the same source can compare unequal depending on declaration order.

P1: `resolve_external_type_ids` still only recurses through `ResolvedPath` and container variants, but the new assoc-item encoder now emits `Type::QualifiedPath` values. Any external crate ids inside `self_type` or `trait_` stay unresolved, so valid trait-scoped defaults like `<ext::Foo as ext::Trait>::Assoc` can compare/resolve incorrectly. See [encoder_state_type_ref_parsing.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec/encoder_state_type_ref_parsing.rs#L32) and [encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec/encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs#L559).

P1: `rewrite_trait_scoped_type_inner` does not recurse into `ImplTrait`, `DynTrait`, `FunctionPointer`, or `Pat`, so trait projections nested inside those forms are left as plain `ResolvedPath` nodes. Inputs like `Vec<fn() -> T::Item>` or `impl Iterator<Item = T::Item>` will be mis-encoded instead of producing the expected qualified projection shape. See [encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec/encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs#L564).

Associated type bounds are still encoded with `encode_and_validate_bound`, which only rewrites bare generic arguments. If a new `assoc_types` entry uses a valid projection in its bound, such as `type Output: Iterator<Item = T::Item>` or `<T as Trait>::Item`, the A-side bound keeps that projection as an unresolved/resolved path instead of the `Type::QualifiedPath` shape rustdoc emits. `build_trait_method_map` then fingerprints A as `#0::Item`/unresolved while C formats `<#0 as _>::Item`, so valid assoc-bearing traits can stay non-blue. The assoc type default path already uses `encode_trait_scoped_type_ref`; bounds need the same trait-scoped projection rewrite and coverage.

`encode_trait_scoped_bound` parses associated-type bounds without the enclosing trait generic context, then tries to rewrite the parsed tree afterward. Because `encode_bound_str`/`parse_generic_bound` resolves single-segment names against locals/std prelude before this rewrite runs, an in-scope generic that shadows a prelude/local name inside a bound argument is already lost. For example, `trait Port<From> { type Out: Iterator<Item = From>; }` should encode `From` as `Type::Generic("From")` to match rustdoc, but this path will first resolve it as the prelude `std::convert::From`, and `rewrite_trait_scoped_bound` cannot recover it. That makes otherwise valid associated-type bounds produce false signal mismatches. Parse bounds with the trait generic names in scope, or otherwise prevent in-scope generic names from being resolved before rewriting.

P1: `encode_trait_scoped_type_ref` now parses nested trait-generic projections like `Vec<T::Item>` through `parse_type_ref_str_with_generics`, but that parser only recognizes bare generic names. During its discovery pass it treats the `T::Item` prefix as an external crate named `T`; the later rewrite converts the type shape to `QualifiedPath` but leaves the fake external crate registered globally in the emitted `Crate`. That can pollute `external_crates` and make subsequent `T::...` references look resolved instead of unresolved. Teach the parser to handle generic-prefix multi-segment paths before external-crate registration, or pre-rewrite these projections without registering the prefix.

P1: This production module is now 748 lines with no test block exemption, exceeding the repository's 700-line maximum for production modules. The new associated-item encoding and trait-scoped rewrite helpers should be split into a sibling module, similar to the `decode.rs` split done for the same size rule.

`try_build_generic_projection` bypasses the normal parser for `T::Assoc`-style associated projections but only validates the first character of the associated item name. Since `TypeRef::new` only rejects empty strings, an in-memory catalogue can now encode invalid Rust type refs such as `T::Item-foo` as `Type::QualifiedPath` instead of returning `InvalidTypeRef`, unlike the other encode paths. Validate the full identifier or fall through to the parser before constructing the qualified path.

`parse_type_ref_str_with_generics` forwards to the normal parser without shadowing local catalogue names. That parser still receives `local_name_to_id`, so a legal generic impl such as `impl<T> Trait for T` is encoded as the local catalogue type `T` whenever the catalogue also declares a type named `T`, instead of `Type::Generic("T")`. Other trait-scoped paths avoid this by temporarily shadowing local names before parsing; this wrapper needs the same precedence or the A-side impl target can mismatch rustdoc for generic impls under a local-name collision.

The new `assoc_types` and `assoc_consts` catalogue wire-format fields are not exercised by the `CatalogueDocumentCodec` tests: there is no decode, encode/round-trip, or invalid-input test covering these DTO fields. Since this is a public JSON schema extension, regressions in serde defaults, newtype validation, duplicate-name rejection, or `default_value` mapping could pass CI. Add focused happy-path and error-case codec tests for associated types and associated consts.

`verify_sibling_json_stages()` returns immediately on any Stage 1 error (`return Some(stage1)`), so the combined `verify()` path never reports Stage 2 findings when `spec.json` is stale or otherwise invalid. This contradicts the briefing requirement that the combined caller merge Stage 1 and Stage 2 outcomes, and it can silently hide type-signal violations behind an earlier spec-adr failure.

P1: `verify_sibling_json_stages` always invokes and merges both stage functions, so a symlink-guard failure on `spec.json` is reported twice (once from each stage). That violates the briefing’s no-double-counting requirement and inflates `VerifyOutcome::error_count()` for a single underlying failure. Short-circuit stage 2 when stage 1 fails on the shared file/symlink preamble, or dedupe identical findings before merging.

`verify_type_signals_from_spec_json` now accepts a `spec_json_path`, but after the symlink/existence guard it never reads or decodes that spec file before evaluating the type-signal catalogues. That means the new standalone Stage 2 entrypoint can return success for a track whose `spec.json` is malformed or unreadable as long as the catalogue signal files are valid, whereas the original combined path failed closed on spec decode errors and the briefing calls for the same shared read/decode preamble in both stage functions. Add the same spec.json read/decode check here before `load_tddd_layers` so `check-impl-catalog` cannot pass with an invalid spec document.

`calc-adr-user` is described as optional here (“薄い validate に留める（または省略）”), but the same ADR later makes the 8-command taxonomy mandatory in AC-01 (`signal {calc,check}-adr-user,...` must exist). Those two requirements conflict, so the command surface is underspecified.

D7 says chain ⓪ implements only `SoTChain` and that calling `calc` for ⓪ is compile-time forbidden, but D1/AC-01 require `signal calc-adr-user` to exist as a no-persist live calculation command. As written, the trait contract and CLI acceptance criteria cannot both be implemented through the stated trait-driven dispatch; clarify whether `calc-adr-user` is outside `PersistedSoTChain` or add a separate no-persist calc contract.

AC-08 contradicts D7's trait contract. D7 defines three traits and repeatedly states that chain ⓪ `AdrUserChain` must implement `LiveSoTChain` so `signal calc-adr-user` has a typed no-persist `calc_live` path, but the acceptance criteria here require only two traits and say `AdrUserChain` implements `SoTChain` only. If implementers follow AC-08, the planned `calc-adr-user` contract from D1/D7 is dropped, so the plan is internally inconsistent.

The ADR keeps `signal render` open as a future namespace option, but the scope briefing says the user explicitly rejected pushing render into the signal namespace. That leaves a rejected API direction alive instead of recording it as rejected, so downstream planning could implement an option this baseline was supposed to rule out.

D7 relies on `PersistedSoTChain: SoTChain` providing the default `SoTChain::check` flow (`load -> check_freshness -> evaluate_gate`), but a Rust subtrait cannot implement a method declared by its supertrait for all implementers. As written, each persisted chain still has to implement `SoTChain::check` separately, so the plan does not actually force freshness checks through the trait surface and the AC-08 compile-time guarantee is not achievable without changing the trait design (for example, a blanket impl or an explicit helper method pattern).

T007 introduces a new separate public Stage2 entrypoint for `signal check-impl-catalog`, but the infrastructure catalogue still only declares `infrastructure::verify::spec_states::verify_from_spec_json` and explicitly says that function is Stage1-only. As written, the plan and the type catalogue disagree about the exposed public surface, so the Stage2 callable is unmodelled in the plan artifacts.

The error contract for invalid strictness values is inconsistent inside the plan. T003 hard-codes an invalid strictness string to `ParseFailed`, but T013 later treats the same case as `ParseFailed` **or** `InvalidValue`, and the infrastructure catalogue already defines `SignalGatesConfigError::InvalidValue`. That leaves the observable failure mode underspecified and makes the earlier module test target disagree with the later one.

`check_catalogue_spec_signals` is described here as taking `CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument` by value, but the plan/spec require the borrowed form `&CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument` (`plan.md:12`, `spec.md:43`). That leaves the generated contract and the implementation plan disagreeing on the API shape, so downstream work could be generated against the wrong ownership model.

T010 removes the old CLI entrypoints before T011 rewires `Makefile.toml` and CI to the new `signal` commands. That leaves an intermediate state where validation can still invoke deleted commands, so the required between-round `cargo make ci-rust` check can fail until the later task lands. Reorder the Makefile/docs migration ahead of the removals, or merge those steps into one task.

`check_strict_merge_gate` is only specified to resolve `SignalGateMatrix::merge_gate.adr_user`, even though the spec says `signal-gates.json` is the chain × gate strictness source of truth and that the merge gate is wired through all four `signal check-* --gate merge` cells. As written, the plan leaves the existing spec-adr/catalog-spec/impl-catalog merge checks hardcoded strict and ignores their config cells, so the declared matrix cannot actually control all four merge-gate chains.

`SpecAdrChain` claims its persistence target is `<track>/<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json`, which is the chain ② per-layer artifact, while the same sentence and the spec define chain ① as using `spec.json`'s `signals` field with a self-consistency check. This contradictory catalogue contract can lead the implementation to read or persist the wrong artifact for `check-spec-adr`.

T010 says the no-alias cleanup is "per CN-06", but CN-06 is the PersistedSoTChain/LiveSoTChain constraint for chain ⓪, not the no-backward-compat requirement. The relevant no-alias requirement is AC-07 / OS-06, so this semantic cross-reference is wrong and breaks the plan traceability for command removal.

The plan places config loading inside `usecase::merge_gate::check_strict_merge_gate` while the loader is declared as `infrastructure::verify::signal_gates_config::load_signal_gates_config`. That forces a usecase-layer function to depend on infrastructure, violating the stated hexagonal layering. Load the file at the CLI/infrastructure boundary or hide it behind a usecase port, then pass a domain `SignalGateMatrix` into the merge-gate logic.

The usecase catalogue says `SpecAdrChain`, `CatalogSpecChain`, and `ImplCatalogChain` obtain `SoTChain` through the `impl<T: PersistedSoTChain> SoTChain for T` blanket impl, but `trait_impls` also declares explicit `domain::SoTChain` impls for those same persisted chains. Following both artifacts would produce conflicting Rust impls; if the entries are only intended as effective impls, the catalogue is semantically misleading. Remove the direct `SoTChain` impl entries for persisted chains or model the blanket impl separately.

`TrackBlobReader::read_adr_verify_report` is declared as requiring a `project_root: PathBuf`, and its docs say it exists to support chain ⓪ evaluation in `check_strict_merge_gate`, but `check_strict_merge_gate` only receives `branch`, `reader`, and `gate_matrix`. The plan therefore has no way for the merge-gate usecase to supply the repo root needed for the ADR directory scan it is required to add, making the chain ⓪ merge-gate task under-specified/infeasible as written.

AC-13 requires unit tests for the new commands, the new trait taxonomy, and config loading, and task coverage marks AC-13 as covered by every task. However the CLI task T009, which is the task that adds all 8 `signal` commands plus aggregate dispatch, contains no test requirement at all; T001 only tests gate resolution helpers, and T013 only covers config loading. As written, the implementation plan can satisfy its listed tasks while still missing the command/dispatch tests explicitly required by AC-13.

`T001` explicitly covers the four trait definitions (`ChainIdentity`, `SoTChain`, `LiveSoTChain`, `PersistedSoTChain`), but the generated table still leaves all four of those entries yellow while only the enums/value objects were promoted to blue. That makes the plan artifacts internally inconsistent about what this round actually landed.

T001 is marked `in_progress`, but its `commit_hash` field was removed instead of remaining explicitly `null`; the same field was also removed from every still-`todo` task. This makes the plan artifact inconsistent with the task-tracking contract that unresolved/in-progress tasks carry a nullable commit slot to be filled when the task is recorded, and it changes T002-T013 even though their semantic state remains `todo`. Restore `"commit_hash": null` for the task entries.

`impl-plan.json` now omits the `commit_hash` field for T002-T013. The plan-artifact task shape and briefing/checklist expect unresolved or in-progress tasks to retain an explicit nullable commit slot (`commit_hash: null`), with only T001 filled after completion. As written, T002 is in progress and T003-T013 are still todo, but their commit slots were removed, making the artifact inconsistent with the task-recording workflow. Restore `"commit_hash": null` for the unresolved task entries.

`task-coverage.json` under-covers AC-14/AC-15: they are only linked to T016/T017, but the Blue transitions still depend on the parser tasks T014/T015 (`QualifiedPath` and `Generic`). As written, the coverage map makes the later catalogue updates look sufficient on their own when they are not.

The blanket-impl where-clause is specified with two incompatible shapes: the ADR/spec prose uses `bound: "domain::PersistedSoTChain"`, while T016 uses `rhs: ["PersistedSoTChain"]`. The plan never reconciles those representations, so the intended JSON shape for `impl_where_predicates` is ambiguous.

`IN-12` / `T015` describe propagating `trait generics + impl_generics` when decoding `methods[].params[].ty` and `methods[].returns`, but the source ADR says the shared context should be `trait generics + method generics` instead. That mismatch leaves method-local generic parameters uncovered and makes the task text inconsistent with its own design reference.

D1 says the catalogue-side `Self::Input<'_>` declaration will be parsed into `Type::QualifiedPath`, and T014 repeats that by asking for a `&Self::Input<'_>` unit test. But the D1 implementation target is specifically `syn::TypePath.qself.is_some()`, which is exercised by fully qualified syntax such as `<Self as ChainIdentity>::Input<'_>`, not plain `Self::Input`. This contradicts the later T016 canonical form and can let T014 pass without testing the new QualifiedPath builder for the actual catalogue form that must become Blue.

`T006` is still marked `in_progress` even though the newly inserted S8 section states that T014-T017 must all complete before T006 resumes. With T014 only in progress and T015-T017 still todo, the plan's active state contradicts its required execution order and can lead the next implementer to continue T006 before the parser/catalogue Blue path is restored.

The execution-order note says the entire S8 group must complete before T006, but the same artifact now marks T006 as done while T015 is still `in_progress`. That leaves the plan self-contradictory about what has to precede T006 and makes the remaining ordering guidance misleading.

D3 is internally inconsistent about the canonical QualifiedPath spelling. The ADR/spec say A-side GAT projections must use `<Self>::Assoc<args>` because `<Self as _>::...` is unparseable, but the same section also says `Some(Path{path:""})` normalizes to `_` and that C-side `<Self as _>::...` and A-side `<Self>::...` converge to the same string. No extra rewrite step is specified, so those claims do not all hold together.

The new associated-item schema is described inconsistently across artifacts. The ADR/spec/plan say `AssocTypeDecl.default` and `AssocConstDecl.default` are `Option<String>`, but `domain-types.json` records `AssocTypeDecl.default` as `Option<TypeRef>` and names the const field `default_value`. That is a concrete contract mismatch between the generated plan artifact and the documented schema.

IN-14 still specifies `AssocTypeDecl.name` and `AssocConstDecl.name` as raw `String` fields, and T017 repeats the same schema, but this same artifact set now catalogs `AssocTypeDecl.name` as `TypeName` and `AssocConstDecl.name` as `AssocConstName` in `domain-types.json`. That is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts: the spec/plan tell implementers to use primitive strings while the rendered domain catalogue records validated identifier newtypes for the implemented design. Align IN-14/T017/ADR D4 with the validated newtype shape so the plan has one authoritative schema.

D1 still describes `<Self as ChainIdentity>::Input<'_>` as the catalogue-side canonical qualified path that will match rustdoc, but D3 later says the catalogue canonical form must be `<Self>::Assoc` because rustdoc Self projections arrive with an empty trait path and explicit `<Self as Trait>::Assoc` will not compare correctly. These are mutually exclusive conventions for the same GAT projection declarations, so the ADR leaves readers with two incompatible implementation rules. Update the D1 text to mark the explicit-trait form as the pre-D3 assumption or align it with the D3 `<Self>::...` convention.

ADR context still says rustdoc/formatter render `Self::Input` as `<Self as ChainIdentity>::Input`, but the later D3 root-cause section says rustdoc emits an empty trait path and the pre-D3 C-side string was `<Self as >::Input`, with the accepted canonical catalogue path being `<Self>::...` rendered as `<Self as _>::...`. These are conflicting descriptions of the same GAT projection behavior, so the ADR leaves readers with two incompatible formatter contracts.

`AC-18` is mapped only to `T017`, but `AC-18` requires the actual `domain-types.json` assoc item declarations and a Blue `calc-impl-catalog` result. `T017` only adds the schema/codecs; the plan puts the catalogue declarations and signal verification in `T018`/later. As written, task coverage claims AC-18 is satisfied by a task that cannot complete the criterion, leaving the acceptance dependency order inconsistent.

The S8 narrative still says T016-T019 are implemented in the worktree but uncommitted and instructs the next operator to commit them in a future order, while the task entries in the same artifact now mark T016-T019 as done with commit hash f3e3b9b5. That leaves the plan asserting both that the work remains uncommitted and that it has already been committed, which can misdirect the next implementation/review step.

`CatalogSpecChain::check_freshness` only compares the document-level `catalogue_declaration_hash` with the current catalogue hash. The chain ② contract requires the persisted per-entry `CatalogueSpecSignal::entry_hash()` values to be checked against freshly computed entry hashes before `evaluate_gate`; otherwise `SoTChain::check` can accept a signals document with stale, missing, or manually trimmed per-entry signals as long as the aggregate declaration hash still matches. Carry the current entry hashes in `CatalogSpecInput` and fail closed on missing/mismatched entries before delegating to `check_catalogue_spec_signals`.

The chain-0 result is only merged at the end of the function, but the early returns here and at the later `stage1.has_errors()` check exit before that merge. Invalid branches or missing/failed `spec.json` therefore drop ADR findings entirely, which contradicts the new requirement that chain ⓪ be accumulated independently of the other stages.

The new `strict=false` arm in `check_chain2_for_layer` is not exercised by any test in this scope, and every existing test now hardcodes `all_strict_matrix()`. That leaves the new warning-mode behavior and the added ADR reader port effectively unverified, so a regression in the non-blocking path could ship unnoticed.

`check_strict_merge_gate` reads the ADR report before calling `validate_branch_ref`, so an untrusted branch string reaches the `TrackBlobReader` port unvalidated. That reintroduces the very ref-safety check this function is supposed to enforce and can make the adapter operate on malicious refs like `..` or `@{...}` before the branch is rejected.

Only `catalog_spec` is exercised with a non-strict matrix; the newly matrix-driven `adr_user`, `spec_adr`, and `impl_catalog` paths all run under `all_strict_matrix()` in tests. A swapped ChainId or accidental hardcoded strict value for any of those cells would still pass the current suite. Add focused interim-mode tests for Chain ⓪, Chain ①, and Chain ③ Yellow signals.

The new Chain ⓪ `read_adr_verify_report` error path is untested. The trait documents `FetchError` as fail-closed and this branch turns it into a blocking finding, but the added tests only cover `NotFound` and `Found` ADR reports. A regression that accidentally skipped ADR scan I/O/parse failures would still pass the current suite. Add a test with an otherwise clean gate and `BlobFetchResult::FetchError` from `read_adr_verify_report`, asserting `has_errors()` and the ADR failure message.

`AssocTypeDecl.name` and `AssocConstDecl.name` are plain `String`s, unlike the rest of `catalogue_v2`'s identifier-bearing fields which use validated newtypes. That lets callers construct invalid associated-item names inside the domain model and defers validation to later layers, which violates the repo's "illegal states unrepresentable" rule for a new public API surface.

`AssocConstName` is a new public validation boundary, but this module adds no direct tests for valid construction, invalid-input rejection, or `Display`/`FromStr` round-trips. Because its validation logic is macro-backed and shared only indirectly with the other identifier wrappers, a regression here would not be caught.

`TraitEntry` now carries `assoc_types` and `assoc_consts`, but there is no test coverage asserting their default-empty construction or that they participate in `PartialEq` the way the existing `generics` and `where_predicates` fields do. That leaves the new trait-item shape under-tested and makes a wiring regression easy to miss.
